title: See every customer
story: Your first mission is to inspect the customer table.
goal: Return all columns from customers.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers;
hint: Use SELECT * when you need every column.

title: Choose specific columns
story: Real analysts avoid pulling columns they do not need.
goal: Return first_name, last_name, and city from customers.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM customers;
hint: List columns after SELECT separated by commas.

title: Filter one state
story: The team wants to focus on Delaware customers.
goal: Show customers where state is DE.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE state = 'DE';

title: Filter by city
story: A local Bear outreach event needs nearby customers.
goal: Show customers from Bear.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Bear';

title: Sort alphabetically
story: Organize a contact list for easy reading.
goal: Show customers ordered by last_name.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT first_name, last_name FROM customers ORDER BY last_name;
hint: ORDER BY goes near the end.

title: Sort highest price
story: Find the most expensive products first.
goal: Show products ordered by price descending.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY price DESC;

title: Limit results
story: A dashboard card only has room for a few rows.
goal: Return only 3 products.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM products LIMIT 3;
hint: LIMIT controls how many rows come back.

title: Use greater than
story: Find customers with strong engagement.
goal: Show customers with loyalty_points greater than 100.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE loyalty_points > 100;

title: Use less than
story: Find low stock products for restocking.
goal: Show products with stock less than 30.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM products WHERE stock < 30;
hint: Use < to find values below a number.

title: Find a range
story: Find mid-priced learning materials.
goal: Show products with price between 10 and 100.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 10 AND 100;

title: Match multiple states
story: Marketing wants customers from DE or PA.
goal: Show customers whose state is DE or PA.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE state IN ('DE','PA');
hint: IN is cleaner than many OR clauses.

title: Use LIKE
story: Find products with SQL in the name.
goal: Show products whose product_name contains SQL.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM products WHERE product_name LIKE '%SQL%';

title: Create an alias
story: Reports should have friendly column names.
goal: Show product_name as item and price as cost.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT product_name AS item, price AS cost FROM products;

title: Calculate line values
story: Revenue needs quantity times price later; practice multiplication.
goal: Show product_name and price * stock as inventory_value.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT product_name, price * stock AS inventory_value FROM products;
hint: SQL can calculate values in SELECT.

title: Distinct categories
story: Build a clean category list.
goal: Show each product category once.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT DISTINCT category FROM products;

title: Count rows
story: Leadership asks how many customers exist.
goal: Return the total number of customers.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_customers FROM customers;

title: Average price
story: Estimate the average product price.
goal: Return average product price.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT AVG(price) AS average_price FROM products;

title: Minimum price
story: Find the cheapest item.
goal: Return the minimum product price.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT MIN(price) AS cheapest FROM products;

title: Maximum price
story: Find the premium item.
goal: Return the maximum product price.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT MAX(price) AS highest_price FROM products;

title: Sum stock
story: Inventory needs a total stock count.
goal: Return total product stock.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT SUM(stock) AS total_stock FROM products;

title: Group by category
story: Inventory should be summarized by category.
goal: Count products in each category.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT category, COUNT(*) AS product_count FROM products GROUP BY category;
hint: Every non-aggregate selected column should be grouped.

title: Filter grouped results
story: Show only categories with multiple products.
goal: Count products by category and keep categories with count greater than 1.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT category, COUNT(*) AS product_count FROM products GROUP BY category HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

title: Combine filters
story: Find Delaware customers with high points.
goal: Show DE customers with loyalty_points above 100.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE state = 'DE' AND loyalty_points > 100;
hint: AND means both conditions must be true.

title: Either condition
story: Find customers in Bear or Dover.
goal: Show customers where city is Bear or Dover.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE city = 'Bear' OR city = 'Dover';
hint: OR means either condition may be true.

title: Not cancelled
story: Reports should ignore cancelled orders.
goal: Show orders that are not cancelled.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE NOT status = 'cancelled';

title: Date filter
story: Review orders after May 10.
goal: Show orders after 2025-05-10.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date > '2025-05-10';
hint: SQLite compares ISO date text well.

title: Order statuses
story: Operations wants to know order status counts.
goal: Count orders by status.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT status, COUNT(*) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY status;
hint: Group by status to get one row per status.

title: Ticket priorities
story: Support wants priority counts.
goal: Count tickets by priority.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT priority, COUNT(*) AS total FROM tickets GROUP BY priority;
hint: COUNT with GROUP BY builds a summary.

title: Open tickets
story: Support needs unresolved tickets.
goal: Show tickets where status is open.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM tickets WHERE status = 'open';
hint: Text value open must be quoted.

title: Completed enrollments
story: Education team needs completed course records.
goal: Show enrollments where completed equals 1.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM enrollments WHERE completed = 1;
hint: SQLite uses 1 for true in this seed data.

title: Scores above 90
story: Find strong student performances.
goal: Show enrollments with score greater than 90.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM enrollments WHERE score > 90;

title: Course difficulty
story: Find beginner courses.
goal: Show beginner courses.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM courses WHERE difficulty = 'beginner';

title: Department list
story: Explore internal teams.
goal: Show all departments.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM departments;

title: Employee salary filter
story: Find employees above a salary threshold.
goal: Show employees with salary above 110000.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > 110000;

title: Project budgets
story: See biggest projects first.
goal: Show projects ordered by budget descending.
steps: Read the goal carefully.
Run the starter SQL.
Edit the query to match the goal.
Use Check Lesson when the result looks right.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM projects ORDER BY budget DESC;

title: Join customers to orders
story: Orders only show customer IDs, so connect names to orders.
goal: Show order_id, first_name, last_name, and status.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT orders.order_id, customers.first_name, customers.last_name, orders.status FROM orders JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id;
hint: JOIN needs ON to explain how tables connect.

title: Join orders to items
story: Order details live in another table.
goal: Show order_id, product_id, and quantity from joined orders and order_items.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT orders.order_id, order_items.product_id, order_items.quantity FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id;

title: Join items to products
story: Product IDs need readable product names.
goal: Show product_name and quantity for order items.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT products.product_name, order_items.quantity FROM order_items JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id;

title: Three table revenue
story: Revenue needs order, item, and product data.
goal: Show order_id and line total using quantity * price.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT orders.order_id, products.product_name, order_items.quantity * products.price AS line_total FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id;

title: Revenue by order
story: Summarize every order total.
goal: Return each order_id and total revenue.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT orders.order_id, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS order_total FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY orders.order_id;

title: Revenue by customer
story: Leadership wants customer revenue.
goal: Return customer names and total revenue.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT customers.first_name, customers.last_name, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS revenue FROM customers JOIN orders ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY customers.customer_id;

title: Left join customers
story: Some customers may have no orders.
goal: Show all customers and their order_id when present.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT customers.first_name, customers.last_name, orders.order_id FROM customers LEFT JOIN orders ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id;
hint: LEFT JOIN keeps the left table rows.

title: Customers without orders
story: Find customers who have not ordered yet.
goal: Show customers with no matching orders.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT customers.* FROM customers LEFT JOIN orders ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id WHERE orders.order_id IS NULL;
hint: After a LEFT JOIN, unmatched right-side values are NULL.

title: Department employees
story: Employee department names live in another table.
goal: Show employee names with department_name.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT employees.first_name, employees.last_name, departments.department_name FROM employees JOIN departments ON employees.department_id = departments.department_id;

title: Average salary by department
story: Compare salary averages across teams.
goal: Return department_name and average salary.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT departments.department_name, AVG(employees.salary) AS avg_salary FROM departments JOIN employees ON departments.department_id = employees.department_id GROUP BY departments.department_id;

title: High average salary
story: Find departments above a salary average.
goal: Return departments with average salary above 110000.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT departments.department_name, AVG(employees.salary) AS avg_salary FROM departments JOIN employees ON departments.department_id = employees.department_id GROUP BY departments.department_id HAVING AVG(employees.salary) > 110000;

title: Self join managers
story: Managers are employees too.
goal: Show employee name and manager first name.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT e.first_name AS employee, m.first_name AS manager FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.employee_id;
hint: Use aliases to join employees to itself.

title: Project department budgets
story: Projects belong to departments.
goal: Show project_name, department_name, and budget.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT projects.project_name, departments.department_name, projects.budget FROM projects JOIN departments ON projects.department_id = departments.department_id;

title: Open projects
story: Some projects have no end date yet.
goal: Show projects where end_date is NULL.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM projects WHERE end_date IS NULL;
hint: Use IS NULL instead of = NULL.

title: Closed ticket duration
story: Support wants days open for closed tickets.
goal: Show ticket_id and julianday(closed_date)-julianday(opened_date) as days_open for closed tickets.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT ticket_id, julianday(closed_date) - julianday(opened_date) AS days_open FROM tickets WHERE closed_date IS NOT NULL;
hint: SQLite can calculate date differences with julianday.

title: Case statement priority
story: Convert priority words into risk scores.
goal: Return ticket_id and a numeric priority_score using CASE.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT ticket_id, CASE priority WHEN 'critical' THEN 4 WHEN 'high' THEN 3 WHEN 'medium' THEN 2 ELSE 1 END AS priority_score FROM tickets;

title: Customer tiers
story: Create loyalty segments.
goal: Return customer names and tier based on loyalty_points.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT first_name, last_name, CASE WHEN loyalty_points >= 200 THEN 'gold' WHEN loyalty_points >= 100 THEN 'silver' ELSE 'bronze' END AS tier FROM customers;

title: Subquery above average
story: Find products priced above the average.
goal: Show products where price is greater than the average price.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products);
hint: A subquery can calculate the average.

title: Subquery customers with orders
story: Find customers who appear in orders.
goal: Show customers whose customer_id is in orders.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE customer_id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders);

title: Exists open tickets
story: Find customers with open support issues.
goal: Show customers where an open ticket exists.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM customers c WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM tickets t WHERE t.customer_id = c.customer_id AND t.status = 'open');

title: CTE order totals
story: Break a revenue query into readable parts.
goal: Use a CTE to calculate order totals.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: WITH order_totals AS (SELECT orders.order_id, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS total FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY orders.order_id) SELECT * FROM order_totals;
hint: WITH creates a temporary named result.

title: CTE high value orders
story: Filter calculated order totals cleanly.
goal: Use a CTE and show orders with total above 100.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: WITH order_totals AS (SELECT orders.order_id, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS total FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY orders.order_id) SELECT * FROM order_totals WHERE total > 100;
hint: Filter the CTE in the outer query.

title: Union city names
story: Build one list of cities from students and customers.
goal: Return unique cities from customers and students.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT city FROM customers UNION SELECT city FROM students;

title: Union all city names
story: Keep every city record from both tables.
goal: Return all city rows from customers and students using UNION ALL.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT city FROM customers UNION ALL SELECT city FROM students;

title: Enrollments with course names
story: Scores need course context.
goal: Show student_id, course_name, and score.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT enrollments.student_id, courses.course_name, enrollments.score FROM enrollments JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.course_id;

title: Student course report
story: Create a student learning report.
goal: Show student names, course_name, and score.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT students.first_name, students.last_name, courses.course_name, enrollments.score FROM enrollments JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.student_id JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.course_id;

title: Average score by subject
story: Compare course subjects by average score.
goal: Return subject and average score.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT courses.subject, AVG(enrollments.score) AS avg_score FROM enrollments JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.course_id GROUP BY courses.subject;

title: Completed rate by course
story: Measure course completion.
goal: Return course_name and sum of completed records.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT courses.course_name, SUM(enrollments.completed) AS completions FROM enrollments JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.course_id GROUP BY courses.course_id;
hint: Because completed is 0 or 1, SUM counts completions.

title: Conditional aggregation
story: Count open and closed tickets in one row.
goal: Return open_count and closed_count using SUM(CASE...).
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'open' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS open_count, SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'closed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS closed_count FROM tickets;
hint: SUM(CASE WHEN condition THEN 1 ELSE 0 END).

title: Multiple joins with filters
story: Find shipped products only.
goal: Show shipped order product names and quantities.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT orders.order_id, products.product_name, order_items.quantity FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id WHERE orders.status = 'shipped';

title: Top customers
story: Find highest loyalty customers.
goal: Show the top 3 customers by loyalty_points.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT first_name, last_name, loyalty_points FROM customers ORDER BY loyalty_points DESC LIMIT 3;

title: Product inventory value by category
story: Inventory value should be grouped.
goal: Return category and total price * stock.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT category, SUM(price * stock) AS inventory_value FROM products GROUP BY category;

title: Discounted line total
story: Discounts reduce line totals.
goal: Show item_id and discounted total using quantity * price * (1 - discount).
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT order_items.item_id, order_items.quantity * products.price * (1 - order_items.discount) AS discounted_total FROM order_items JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id;

title: Having revenue over 100
story: Filter customer revenue after grouping.
goal: Return customers with revenue over 100.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT customers.customer_id, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS revenue FROM customers JOIN orders ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY customers.customer_id HAVING revenue > 100;
hint: SQLite lets HAVING use the alias revenue.

title: Null handling
story: Replace missing end dates for reports.
goal: Show project_name and COALESCE(end_date, 'Active') as status_date.
steps: Identify which tables are needed.
Run the starter SQL.
Check joins, filters, grouping, or expressions.
Read any error message and fix one clause at a time.
starter_sql: SELECT project_name, COALESCE(end_date, 'Active') AS status_date FROM projects;
hint: COALESCE returns the first non-null value.

title: Rank products by price
story: Advanced reports often rank rows without collapsing them.
goal: Show product_name, category, price, and rank by price descending.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT product_name, category, price, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY price DESC) AS price_rank FROM products;

title: Rank products within category
story: Rank products inside each category.
goal: Use RANK partitioned by category ordered by price desc.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT product_name, category, price, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC) AS category_rank FROM products;
hint: PARTITION BY restarts the rank per group.

title: Running revenue by order
story: Show a running total across orders.
goal: Calculate running revenue ordered by order_id.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: WITH order_totals AS (SELECT orders.order_id, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS total FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY orders.order_id) SELECT order_id, total, SUM(total) OVER (ORDER BY order_id) AS running_total FROM order_totals;

title: Customer order sequence
story: Number every customer's orders.
goal: Show customer_id, order_id, and row_number over each customer.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT customer_id, order_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS order_number FROM orders;

title: Previous order date
story: Compare each order to the previous order.
goal: Use LAG(order_date) over customer_id.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT customer_id, order_id, order_date, LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) AS previous_order_date FROM orders;
hint: LAG looks backward in a window.

title: Percent of category stock
story: Find each product's share of category stock.
goal: Show product_name and stock divided by category stock total.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT product_name, category, stock, ROUND(1.0 * stock / SUM(stock) OVER (PARTITION BY category), 3) AS stock_share FROM products;

title: Dense salary rank
story: Rank salaries without gaps.
goal: Show employees with dense rank by salary descending.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT first_name, last_name, salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_rank FROM employees;
hint: DENSE_RANK is similar to RANK but removes gaps.

title: Department salary percentile
story: Divide salaries into buckets.
goal: Use NTILE(4) over salary descending.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT first_name, last_name, salary, NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS salary_quartile FROM employees;

title: CTE chain
story: Complex analysis can use multiple CTEs.
goal: Use two CTEs to calculate shipped revenue by customer.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: WITH shipped_orders AS (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'shipped'), revenue AS (SELECT shipped_orders.customer_id, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS total FROM shipped_orders JOIN order_items ON shipped_orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY shipped_orders.customer_id) SELECT * FROM revenue;
hint: Separate filtering and aggregation into named CTEs.

title: Anti join
story: Find products that have never appeared in order_items.
goal: Use LEFT JOIN and IS NULL to find unordered products.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT products.* FROM products LEFT JOIN order_items ON products.product_id = order_items.product_id WHERE order_items.product_id IS NULL;
hint: This is called an anti join pattern.

title: Correlated subquery
story: Compare each employee to their department average.
goal: Show employees earning more than their department average.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT * FROM employees e WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.department_id = e.department_id);
hint: The inner query references the outer query.

title: Recursive CTE sample
story: Generate numbers using SQL only.
goal: Use a recursive CTE to produce numbers 1 through 5.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: WITH RECURSIVE nums(n) AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT n + 1 FROM nums WHERE n < 5) SELECT * FROM nums;
hint: Recursive CTEs need a starting query and recursive query.

title: View-style CTE report
story: Build a clean report output.
goal: Use a CTE and return customer revenue sorted descending.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: WITH customer_revenue AS (SELECT customers.customer_id, customers.first_name, customers.last_name, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS revenue FROM customers JOIN orders ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY customers.customer_id) SELECT * FROM customer_revenue ORDER BY revenue DESC;
hint: A CTE can act like a temporary report table.

title: Data cleaning trim
story: Imported text can have spaces.
goal: Use TRIM on a text value or column alias.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT first_name, TRIM(last_name) AS clean_last_name FROM customers;
hint: TRIM removes leading and trailing spaces.

title: Data cleaning lower
story: Normalize text for matching.
goal: Show customer emails style by lowercasing first and last names.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT LOWER(first_name || '.' || last_name || '@example.com') AS email FROM customers;

title: String length check
story: Validate text length.
goal: Show product_name and LENGTH(product_name).
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT product_name, LENGTH(product_name) AS name_length FROM products;

title: Bucket prices
story: Create price bands for analytics.
goal: Classify products as low, medium, or high price.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT product_name, CASE WHEN price < 20 THEN 'low' WHEN price < 80 THEN 'medium' ELSE 'high' END AS price_band FROM products;

title: Pivot-like status counts
story: Create dashboard counts across columns.
goal: Count shipped, pending, and cancelled orders in one query.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'shipped' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS shipped, SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'pending' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS pending, SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled FROM orders;
hint: Conditional aggregation is a SQL dashboard superpower.

title: Nested aggregation
story: Find categories above the overall average category inventory value.
goal: Use a subquery over grouped category inventory values.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: WITH category_values AS (SELECT category, SUM(price * stock) AS value FROM products GROUP BY category) SELECT * FROM category_values WHERE value > (SELECT AVG(value) FROM category_values);

title: Date month grouping
story: Group orders by month.
goal: Use substr(order_date,1,7) and count orders.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT substr(order_date, 1, 7) AS order_month, COUNT(*) AS total_orders FROM orders GROUP BY substr(order_date, 1, 7);
hint: YYYY-MM strings work well for month grouping.

title: Open ticket aging
story: Estimate how long open tickets have been open.
goal: Use julianday('2025-05-20') - julianday(opened_date) for open tickets.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT ticket_id, julianday('2025-05-20') - julianday(opened_date) AS age_days FROM tickets WHERE status = 'open';
hint: Use a fixed date for repeatable training data.

title: Cross join scenario
story: Generate product-course combinations for bundle ideas.
goal: Use CROSS JOIN between products and courses with a small LIMIT.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT products.product_name, courses.course_name FROM products CROSS JOIN courses LIMIT 10;

title: Explain duplicate issue
story: Bad joins create duplicate or inflated counts.
goal: Join orders to order_items and count item rows by status.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT orders.status, COUNT(order_items.item_id) AS item_rows FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id GROUP BY orders.status;
hint: Counting joined rows counts items, not orders.

title: Count distinct orders
story: Fix inflated counts caused by joins.
goal: Count distinct orders by status after joining order_items.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT orders.status, COUNT(DISTINCT orders.order_id) AS order_count FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id GROUP BY orders.status;

title: Multi-condition CASE
story: Build a simple risk flag for tickets.
goal: Mark open high or critical tickets as urgent.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT ticket_id, CASE WHEN status = 'open' AND priority IN ('high','critical') THEN 'urgent' ELSE 'normal' END AS risk_flag FROM tickets;
hint: CASE can include AND and IN.

title: Subquery in FROM
story: Treat a query result like a table.
goal: Use a derived table to average order totals.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT AVG(total) AS average_order_total FROM (SELECT orders.order_id, SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) AS total FROM orders JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY orders.order_id) totals;
hint: Derived tables must have an alias.

title: Window plus filter CTE
story: Find each category's most expensive product.
goal: Use RANK in a CTE and filter rank = 1.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: WITH ranked AS (SELECT product_name, category, price, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY price DESC) AS rnk FROM products) SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE rnk = 1;
hint: Window results are easiest to filter in a CTE.

title: Salary difference from department avg
story: Compare employees to their team average.
goal: Show salary minus department average using AVG OVER.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT first_name, last_name, salary, salary - AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department_id) AS diff_from_department_avg FROM employees;
hint: Window AVG keeps each employee row.

title: Completion rate
story: Calculate percent completion by subject.
goal: Return subject and AVG(completed) as completion_rate.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT courses.subject, ROUND(AVG(enrollments.completed), 2) AS completion_rate FROM enrollments JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.course_id GROUP BY courses.subject;

title: Final boss report
story: Build a full business report with joins, grouping, sorting, and calculations.
goal: Return customer name, order count, revenue, and tier, sorted by revenue descending.
steps: Break the problem into smaller pieces.
Use aliases to keep SQL readable.
Run the query before checking the lesson.
When an error appears, inspect clause order and table references.
starter_sql: SELECT customers.first_name || ' ' || customers.last_name AS customer_name, COUNT(DISTINCT orders.order_id) AS order_count, ROUND(SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price * (1 - order_items.discount)), 2) AS revenue, CASE WHEN SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) >= 200 THEN 'gold' WHEN SUM(order_items.quantity * products.price) >= 100 THEN 'silver' ELSE 'bronze' END AS tier FROM customers JOIN orders ON customers.customer_id = orders.customer_id JOIN order_items ON orders.order_id = order_items.order_id JOIN products ON order_items.product_id = products.product_id GROUP BY customers.customer_id ORDER BY revenue DESC;
hint: Combine everything: joins, aggregates, CASE, and ordering.
